walkthrough.title: Lay Out the Sultan Tiles
walkthrough.text: Let's set up Five Tribes. First, find all 30 Sultan tiles. These are the double-sided square tiles that make up the Sultanate of Naqala. Shuffle them all face down, then lay them face up in a five by six grid. You should end up with a big rectangle of tiles showing oases, villages, markets, sacred places, and other locations.

walkthrough.title: Fill the Bag with Meeples
walkthrough.text: Now gather all 90 wooden meeples. There are five colors: yellow Viziers, white Elders, green Merchants, blue Builders, and red Assassins. Drop every single one into the cloth bag and give it a thorough shake to mix them up.

walkthrough.title: Populate the Grid
walkthrough.text: This is the fun part. Reach into the bag and draw three random meeples. Place them on the first tile in your grid. Then draw three more for the next tile. Keep going until every one of the 30 tiles has exactly three meeples sitting on it. The board should look colorful and chaotic.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Resource Market
walkthrough.text: Find the deck of 54 resource cards. These show goods like Ivory, Jewels, Gold, Papyrus, Silk, Spices, Fish, Wheat, and Pottery. Give the deck a good shuffle and place it face down near the board. Then flip the top nine cards face up in a row next to the deck. This is the market.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Djinn Display
walkthrough.text: Find the 22 Djinn cards. These are the cards with magical beings on them that grant special powers. Shuffle them face down and place them in a pile near the board. Then flip the top three Djinn cards face up next to the pile. These are the Djinns available for purchase.

walkthrough.title: Set Out Palm Trees and Palaces
walkthrough.text: Place all the wooden palm tree tokens in one pile and all the wooden palace tokens in another pile near the board. These will be placed on tiles during the game. Palm trees are worth three victory points each and palaces are worth five.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Coin Supply
walkthrough.text: Place all the gold coins near the board as a central supply. There are coins in one and five denominations. Make sure everyone can reach them easily. Coins are very important because each coin is worth one victory point at the end of the game.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Turn Order Track
walkthrough.text: Place the turn order and bid track near the board where everyone can see it. This track is where players will bid coins to determine who goes first each round. Put the bid markers nearby as well.

walkthrough.title: Hand Out Player Components
walkthrough.text: Each player takes eight camels in their chosen player color and a turn order marker. The camels are used to claim tiles on the board when you empty them of meeples. Keep your camels in a personal supply near you.

walkthrough.title: Distribute Starting Coins
walkthrough.text: Each player takes 50 coins from the supply. This is your starting wealth. Very important rule: keep your coin total hidden from other players. Cup your hand around them or hide them behind a screen. Coins are secret information in Five Tribes.

walkthrough.title: Determine First Round Order
walkthrough.text: For the very first round, randomly determine the turn order. You can draw lots, roll a die, or use any method your group likes. Starting from round two onward, players will bid coins to determine turn order. The board is now ready to play.

summary.title: Distribute Starting Coins

summary.title: Determine First Round Order
bullets: Randomly determine turn order for round 1
From round 2 onward, players bid coins for turn order

walkthrough.title: Round Structure Overview
walkthrough.text: Five Tribes plays over multiple rounds until an end condition triggers. Each round has two phases. First, all players bid coins to determine turn order. Second, each player takes a turn in the order they bid. After everyone has taken a turn, refill the market and Djinn display, and start a new round.

walkthrough.title: Bidding for Turn Order
walkthrough.text: At the start of each round, all players simultaneously decide how many coins to bid. You can bid zero if you want to save money. Place your bid secretly, then reveal simultaneously. The highest bidder goes first, the second highest goes second, and so on. Ties are broken by whoever was earlier in the previous turn order. Remember, every coin you bid is spent and goes back to the supply. Since coins are victory points, overbidding can cost you the game.

walkthrough.title: The Mancala Move
walkthrough.text: This is the heart of Five Tribes. On your turn, pick any tile on the board and pick up all the meeples on it. Then move in a straight line or change direction at each tile, dropping one meeple per tile as you go. You move orthogonally only, meaning up, down, left, or right. No diagonals allowed. You cannot backtrack to the tile you just came from.

walkthrough.title: The Last Meeple Rule
walkthrough.text: Here is the most important rule in the game. The very last meeple you drop must land on a tile that already has at least one meeple of the same color on it. If you cannot make a legal drop where the last meeple matches a color on the destination tile, that move is illegal. This rule is what makes every move a puzzle.

walkthrough.title: Collect Your Tribe
walkthrough.text: After dropping your last meeple, look at the color of that last meeple. Now pick up all meeples of that same color from the tile, including the one you just dropped. These meeples are your tribe for this turn and they determine what action you get.

walkthrough.title: Vizier Action
walkthrough.text: If you collected yellow Viziers, keep them in front of you for the rest of the game. At the end of the game, each Vizier is worth ten victory points. But there is a huge bonus on top: you also score ten bonus points for each opponent who has fewer Viziers than you. In a four-player game, holding the majority can mean 30 extra points.

walkthrough.title: Elder Action
walkthrough.text: If you collected white Elders, keep them in front of you. Each Elder is worth two victory points at the end of the game. Elders also serve as currency for buying Djinns. When you visit a Sacred Place tile, you can spend Elders to claim a Djinn card from the display.

walkthrough.title: Merchant Action
walkthrough.text: If you collected green Merchants, take resource cards from the front of the market row equal to the number of Merchants you collected. For example, if you picked up two Merchants, take the first two cards from the market row. Resource cards are sold in sets of unique goods for coins, and the value scales dramatically with set size.

walkthrough.title: Builder Action
walkthrough.text: If you collected blue Builders, you earn coins immediately. Count the number of blue point-value icons on surrounding tiles and the tile you landed on. Multiply that count by the number of Builders you collected. That total is how many coins you take from the supply. Builders near clusters of high-value tiles can generate massive payouts.

walkthrough.title: Assassin Action
walkthrough.text: If you collected red Assassins, you can kill one meeple. The number of Assassins you collected equals the range in tiles. Choose one meeple on a tile within that range and remove it from the game permanently. Alternatively, you can kill one Vizier or one Elder from another player's collection. Assassins are both offensive and strategic.

walkthrough.title: Tile Actions
walkthrough.text: After resolving your tribe action, you also perform the action of the tile where your last meeple landed. Oasis tiles let you place a palm tree. Village tiles let you place a palace. Small Markets let you draw three resource cards and keep one. Large Markets let you draw six and keep two. Sacred Places let you spend Elders to buy a Djinn from the display.

walkthrough.title: Claiming Tiles
walkthrough.text: If your mancala move leaves the final tile completely empty of meeples, you may place one of your camels on it to claim it. You now own that tile and will score its printed victory point value at the end of the game. Any palm trees or palaces on your claimed tiles also score for you. Claiming tiles is a major source of points.

walkthrough.title: Selling Resources
walkthrough.text: Let's talk about the resource economy. You can sell resource cards at any point during your turn for coins. The key is that larger sets of unique goods are worth exponentially more. One unique card is worth one coin. Two unique cards are worth three. Three unique are worth seven. It goes all the way up to nine unique cards for 60 coins. Hold for bigger sets when you can.

walkthrough.title: Djinn Powers
walkthrough.text: Djinns are powerful cards purchased at Sacred Places by spending two Elders. Each Djinn has a unique ongoing power that can bend the rules of the game. Some give you extra victory points for specific tile types. Some protect you from Assassins. Some grant bonus actions or special movement. Each Djinn also has printed victory points scored at game end.

walkthrough.title: Game End Conditions
walkthrough.text: The game ends when one of two things happens. Either a player places their last camel on the board, or no player can make a legal mancala move. When this triggers, finish the current round so that every player who bid gets a turn. Then move to final scoring.

walkthrough.title: Final Scoring
walkthrough.text: Add up points from eight categories. First, one point per coin. Second, ten points per Vizier plus ten bonus per opponent with fewer Viziers. Third, two points per Elder. Fourth, printed points on claimed tiles. Fifth, three points per palm tree on your tiles. Sixth, five points per palace on your tiles. Seventh, Djinn card points. Eighth, sell all remaining resource cards and convert to coins. The player with the most total points wins. Tiebreaker goes to the most Viziers plus Elders combined.

summary.title: Round Structure Overview
bullets: Multiple rounds until end condition triggers
Each round: bid for turn order → take turns → refill market/Djinns

walkthrough.title: Welcome to Naqala
walkthrough.text: Welcome to your first game of Five Tribes! In this practice tutorial, we will walk through a few rounds with example players to show you how the mancala movement and tribe actions work together. Just follow along with the moves, even if your board looks different. Ready? Let's go.

walkthrough.title: Round One Bidding
walkthrough.text: Let's start with the bidding phase. Player 1 bids three coins, Player 2 bids one coin, Player 3 bids zero coins, and Player 4 bids five coins. Player 4 goes first since they bid the most. Player 1 goes second with three. Player 2 goes third with one. Player 3 goes last with zero. Notice Player 4 spent five victory points just to go first.

walkthrough.title: Player Four's Opening Move
walkthrough.text: Player 4 scans the board for a good opening. They spot a tile with two green Merchants and one blue Builder. They pick up all three meeples. Moving to the right, they drop the Builder on the first tile. Moving right again, they drop one Merchant on a tile that already has a green meeple. The last green Merchant lands on a tile with another green meeple. Legal move!

walkthrough.title: Resolving the Tribe Action
walkthrough.text: Player 4's last meeple was green, and there was one more green meeple already on that tile. They pick up both green Merchants. That is two Merchants collected, so Player 4 takes the first two resource cards from the market row. They grab Ivory and Gold. Nice start to a resource set.

walkthrough.title: The Tile Action and Claiming
walkthrough.text: The tile where Player 4 landed is a Small Market. That means they also get to draw three resource cards from the deck and keep one. Player 4 draws Silk, Fish, and Papyrus, and keeps Silk. Even better, the tile is now completely empty of meeples, so Player 4 places a camel on it to claim it.

walkthrough.title: Player One's Turn: Vizier Collection
walkthrough.text: Player 1 spots a tile with two yellow Viziers and a white Elder. They pick up all three meeples. Moving one tile down, they drop the Elder. Moving one more tile down, they drop one Vizier onto a tile with another yellow meeple. Last Vizier drops and matches. Player 1 collects both yellow Viziers and keeps them in their scoring area.

walkthrough.title: Why Viziers Matter
walkthrough.text: Let's talk about why Player 1 targeted Viziers. Each Vizier is worth ten points, but the majority bonus is where the real value lives. If Player 1 ends the game with more Viziers than all three opponents, that is 30 bonus points on top of the per-Vizier value. Collecting Viziers early establishes a lead that opponents must either match or concede.

walkthrough.title: Player Two's Turn: Builder Coins
walkthrough.text: Player 2 finds a tile with three blue Builders near a cluster of high-value tiles. They pick up and sow the meeples, ending on a tile surrounded by four tiles with blue point icons. With three Builders and five counted icons, Player 2 earns 15 coins from the supply. That is 15 victory points in one move.

walkthrough.title: Player Three's Turn: The Mistake
walkthrough.text: Player 3 spots a Sacred Place tile and wants to buy a Djinn. They make a mancala move ending with white Elders near the Sacred Place. They collect two Elders and spend them immediately on a Djinn. But here is the problem: Player 3 bid zero coins to save money, yet spent both Elders right away. Now they have a Djinn but no Elders left for future Djinn purchases and only two victory points from the Djinn itself.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Plan Your Elder Economy
walkthrough.text: The mistake here is spending Elders too eagerly. Elders serve double duty: they are worth two victory points each AND they are the currency for Djinns. If you spend all your Elders early on a mediocre Djinn, you might miss a game-changing Djinn that appears later. Collect a few Elders first, evaluate the Djinn display, and then make a purchase when the right one appears.

walkthrough.title: Round Two Bidding
walkthrough.text: Time for round two bidding. Player 4, having seen a great move on the board, bids eight coins. Player 1 bids two. Player 2 bids zero. Player 3 bids one. Player 4 goes first again but has now spent 13 coins total on bidding. That is 13 victory points gone just for turn order.

walkthrough.title: Overbidding Mistake
walkthrough.text: Let's stop and talk about Player 4's bidding. Going first is powerful because you get the best move on the board. But spending eight coins means that move needs to generate more than eight coins worth of value just to break even. Most moves do not do that. A good rule of thumb: bid just enough to get a good position, not necessarily first place. Zero bids are perfectly fine when the board state is open.

walkthrough.title: Assassin Demonstration
walkthrough.text: Player 1 makes a move collecting three red Assassins. That gives them a range of three tiles. They look across the board and spot Player 4's claimed tile with a lone meeple on an adjacent tile. Player 1 uses the Assassin action to remove that meeple from the game. Now there is one fewer meeple near Player 4's territory.

walkthrough.title: Alternative Assassin Use
walkthrough.text: But wait, Player 1 could have used those Assassins differently. Instead of killing a board meeple, they could have targeted Player 4's Vizier collection, removing one Vizier from Player 4. That would swing the Vizier majority. Assassins aimed at opponent Viziers can swing 20 points: ten points your opponent loses, plus the majority shifting.

walkthrough.title: Illegal Move Mistake
walkthrough.text: Player 3 picks up three meeples from a tile and starts moving. They drop a blue meeple on a tile, then a yellow one on the next tile. But the last meeple is red and the destination tile has only white and green meeples. No red meeple to match! This move is illegal. Player 3 must pick up their meeples and choose a completely different move.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Always Check the Destination
walkthrough.text: Before picking up meeples, always look at where your last meeple will land and check whether its color matches something already there. New players often plan the pickup without checking the landing. Trace your entire move mentally before you touch any meeples. This prevents illegal moves and wasted time.

walkthrough.title: Mid-Game Board State
walkthrough.text: After several rounds, the board is starting to thin out. Some tiles have camels on them. Others have only one or two meeples left. The market has been partially depleted and refilled. Two Djinns have been claimed and the display has fresh ones. This is when the game gets really interesting because every move becomes more constrained and more valuable.

walkthrough.title: Resource Set Selling
walkthrough.text: Player 2 has been quietly collecting resource cards. They now hold Ivory, Jewels, Gold, Silk, and Fish. That is five unique goods, worth 21 coins when sold as a set. Compare that to selling them individually for five coins total. Player 2 cashes in the full set for 21 coins. Patience with resource sets pays off enormously.

walkthrough.title: Continue Playing
walkthrough.text: Play continues with each player bidding, performing mancala moves, collecting tribes, executing tile actions, and claiming empty tiles. The board gets sparser each round. Keep collecting Viziers for the majority bonus, pick up Elders for Djinn purchases, and watch for Builder hotspots near tile clusters.

walkthrough.title: Game End Approaching
walkthrough.text: The game ends when a player places their last camel or no legal move exists. As the board thins, start thinking about final scoring. Count your Viziers relative to opponents. Consider whether to sell resources now or hold for one more card. Make sure you have claimed tiles with palm trees and palaces for maximum bonus points.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: Congratulations! You have seen the core mechanics of Five Tribes in action. You have watched the mancala movement, resolved tribe actions, learned about tile claiming, and seen three common mistakes. Remember: always check the last meeple rule before moving, do not overbid, and plan your Elder economy carefully.

walkthrough.title: Before You Begin
walkthrough.text:
Before you dive into a full game, here are three things that can help.

First, check out General Tips for strategy basics and common pitfalls to avoid.

Second, reference the Appendix for a quick guide to tribe actions, tile types, and resource values.

And most importantly, ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun!

summary.title: Before You Begin
bullets: Check out General Tips → strategy basics for beginners
Reference the Appendix → quick-reference for tribes and tiles
Ask your personal GM Guide → tap Q&A for instant answers during play

walkthrough.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
walkthrough.text: Before diving into strategy, here are the most common mistakes new players make. First, overbidding for turn order. Every coin spent bidding is a victory point gone forever. Second, forgetting the last meeple rule and making illegal moves. Third, ignoring the Vizier majority race. Fourth, selling resource cards one at a time instead of building sets. Keep these in mind and you will avoid the biggest beginner traps.

walkthrough.title: Bidding Strategy
walkthrough.text: Going first gives you the best move, but the cost must be justified. A good approach is to bid modestly in the early rounds when many good moves exist and everyone is exploring. Save aggressive bids for moments when you spot a game-changing move that you must have before an opponent takes it. Bidding zero is perfectly valid and keeps all those coins as victory points.

walkthrough.title: The Vizier Race
walkthrough.text: The Vizier majority bonus is one of the largest point swings in the game. In a four-player game, having the most Viziers can net you 30 bonus points, while the player with the fewest gets zero bonus. Start collecting Viziers early. If you fall behind, consider using Assassins to remove Viziers from the leader. Do not let one player run away with the Vizier majority unchallenged.

walkthrough.title: Reading the Board
walkthrough.text: The key skill in Five Tribes is reading the board before your mancala move. Look for clusters of same-colored meeples because they lead to powerful tribe actions. Check which tiles will be left empty so you can claim them. Scan for Builder hotspots near high-value tiles. And always trace your planned move mentally to verify the last meeple matches before you touch anything.

walkthrough.title: Resource Set Management
walkthrough.text: Resource cards scale exponentially with set size. A single card is worth one coin, but nine unique cards are worth 60. The jump from four unique to five unique is eight coins. This means you should almost never sell individual cards. Hold resources and aim for sets of at least five unique goods before selling. If you can collect Merchants regularly, resource selling becomes a dominant strategy.

walkthrough.title: Djinn Selection
walkthrough.text: Not all Djinns are created equal, and their value depends heavily on your current strategy and the board state. Djinns that grant ongoing passive bonuses are usually better than one-time effects. Look for Djinns that multiply the value of what you are already doing. For example, if you have been claiming tiles, a Djinn that grants extra points per claimed tile is worth far more to you than to someone else. Always evaluate Djinns relative to your game plan.

walkthrough.title: Tile Claiming Priority
walkthrough.text: Claimed tiles score their printed value plus any palm trees and palaces on them. A tile worth five points with two palm trees and a palace is worth 16 points total. Focus on claiming tiles with high printed values and those that already have or can receive palms and palaces. Oasis and Village tile actions let you add palms and palaces, so plan your moves to place these before claiming adjacent tiles.

walkthrough.title: Assassin Timing
walkthrough.text: Assassins are the most versatile tribe in the game. They can remove meeples from the board to empty tiles for claiming. They can kill opponent Viziers to swing the majority. They can eliminate key meeples that opponents are eyeing for their next move. Use Assassins defensively when an opponent is about to make a devastating move, and offensively when you need to empty a tile for claiming or disrupt a Vizier lead.

summary.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
bullets: Overbidding = spending VP for turn order
Forgetting the last meeple matching rule = illegal moves
Ignoring Vizier majority = conceding 30+ point bonus
Selling resources individually instead of in sets

summary.title: Bidding Strategy
bullets: Bid modestly early; save aggressive bids for game-changing moves
Going first is powerful but must justify the VP cost
Bidding zero is valid and preserves VP
In later rounds, bid more when the board has fewer good options

summary.title: The Vizier Race
bullets: Majority bonus = 10 VP per opponent with fewer Viziers
4-player game: majority can net 30 bonus VP
Start collecting early; use Assassins to disrupt leader
Never let one player monopolize Viziers unchallenged

summary.title: Reading the Board
bullets: Look for clusters of same-color meeples for big tribe actions
Check which tiles will be emptied for claiming
Scan for Builder hotspots near high-value tile clusters
Trace full moves mentally before touching meeples

summary.title: Resource Set Management
bullets: Exponential scaling: 1=1, 3=7, 5=21, 7=40, 9=60 coins
Never sell individual cards — hold for sets of 5+
Merchant-heavy strategy = dominant when market is stocked
Sell mid-game if you need coins for critical bids

summary.title: Djinn Selection
bullets: Ongoing passive bonuses > one-time effects
Choose Djinns that multiply your existing strategy
Evaluate relative to your game plan, not in isolation
Some Djinns are game-changing; plan Sacred Place visits

summary.title: Tile Claiming Priority
bullets: Claimed tile VP + palms (3 VP each) + palaces (5 VP each)
Prioritize high-printed-value tiles with room for palms/palaces
Use Oasis and Village tile actions to add tokens before claiming
Territories with multiple claimed tiles = higher total value

summary.title: Assassin Timing
bullets: Empty tiles for claiming by killing the last meeple
Target opponent Viziers to swing majority bonus
Disrupt opponents' planned moves by removing key meeples
Defensive and offensive uses both valid

walkthrough.title: Vizier Monopoly Strategy
walkthrough.text: The most aggressive strategy in Five Tribes is cornering the Vizier market. If you can collect seven or eight Viziers in a four-player game, you will score 70 to 80 points from Viziers alone plus 30 bonus points for having more than all three opponents. The trick is to start early, bid aggressively when Vizier-rich tiles are available, and use Assassins to steal Viziers from anyone who contests you.

walkthrough.title: Merchant Engine Strategy
walkthrough.text: The Merchant engine focuses on collecting resource cards through green Merchants and market tile actions. The key is to build toward the nine unique goods set worth 60 coins. Even hitting seven unique goods for 40 coins is transformative. Focus your mancala moves on collecting three or four Merchants at once to grab multiple cards per turn. Visit Large Markets whenever possible because drawing six cards and keeping two dramatically accelerates your set building.

walkthrough.title: Builder Coin Engine
walkthrough.text: Builders are the fastest way to generate raw coins. The formula is number of Builders multiplied by number of surrounding blue value icons. Position your moves near dense clusters of tiles with blue icons. Three Builders next to five blue icons is 15 coins in a single action. Some Djinns amplify Builder payouts even further. This strategy converts immediately to victory points since every coin equals one point.

walkthrough.title: Territory Control Strategy
walkthrough.text: Claiming tiles is not just about the printed value. It is about controlling territory. When you claim adjacent tiles, you build a network that can be enhanced with palm trees and palaces. A cluster of three claimed tiles with palms and palaces on each can be worth 40 to 50 points. Focus your mancala moves on emptying tiles near your existing claims. Combine Assassin actions with tile claiming to expand your territory efficiently.

walkthrough.title: Djinn Synergy Combos
walkthrough.text: The strongest games often revolve around Djinn synergies. Some Djinns reward specific tile types, so combining them with a territory control strategy amplifies your scoring. Others modify movement rules, letting you reach meeple clusters that other players cannot access. Study the Djinn display each round and grab Djinns that compound your existing advantages. Two synergistic Djinns are worth more than three unrelated ones.

walkthrough.title: Assassin Disruption Strategy
walkthrough.text: Assassins can be used purely offensively to disrupt opponents. Target the player in the Vizier lead by removing their Viziers. Empty tiles near opponent claims before they can expand. Remove key meeples that an opponent needs for their next move. This strategy does not generate points directly for you, but it prevents opponents from scoring. In a close game, denial is as valuable as scoring.

walkthrough.title: Adaptive Bidding
walkthrough.text: Advanced players adjust their bidding based on board state and opponent behavior. If the board has one clearly dominant move, bid just enough to secure it. If the board is open with many good options, bid zero and save coins. Track opponents' approximate coin totals throughout the game. Near the end game, a well-timed high bid for the perfect closing move can be worth the investment.

walkthrough.title: Endgame Timing
walkthrough.text: You can control when the game ends by managing your camel supply. If you are ahead, push to end the game quickly by claiming tiles aggressively. If you are behind, slow down and avoid claiming tiles to give yourself more rounds to catch up. Pay attention to opponents' remaining camels too. If someone is about to place their last camel, you know the game is ending and can plan your final moves accordingly.

summary.title: Vizier Monopoly Strategy
bullets: Corner the Vizier market: 7-8 Viziers = 70-80 VP + 30 bonus
Start collecting early, bid aggressively for Vizier-rich tiles
Use Assassins to steal Viziers from contesting opponents
Forces opponents into a costly arms race or concession

summary.title: Merchant Engine Strategy
bullets: Build toward 9 unique goods (60 coins) or 7+ unique (40+ coins)
Collect 3-4 Merchants per move for multiple cards
Large Markets draw 6 keep 2 = fastest set acceleration
Sell at optimal set sizes for maximum payout

summary.title: Builder Coin Engine
bullets: Coins = VP immediately; Builders near tile clusters = massive payouts
Formula: # Builders x # surrounding blue VP icons
3 Builders x 5 icons = 15 coins per action
Some Djinns amplify Builder payouts further

summary.title: Territory Control Strategy
bullets: Claim adjacent tiles to build scoring networks
Enhance claimed tiles with palms (3 VP) and palaces (5 VP)
3-tile cluster with enhancements = 40-50 VP
Combine Assassin actions with emptying tiles for claims

summary.title: Djinn Synergy Combos
bullets: Djinns that reward your existing strategy = high value
Movement-modifying Djinns open unreachable meeple clusters
Two synergistic Djinns > three unrelated Djinns
Study display each round, grab compounding advantages

summary.title: Assassin Disruption Strategy
bullets: Remove opponent Viziers to swing majority bonus
Empty tiles near opponent claims to block expansion
Remove key meeples opponents need for next move
Denial = scoring in a close game

summary.title: Adaptive Bidding
bullets: One dominant move on board = bid just enough to secure it
Many good options = bid zero, save coins for VP

Well-timed high bid for a perfect closing move = worthwhile

summary.title: Endgame Timing
bullets: Control game pace via camel placement speed
Ahead = claim aggressively to end quickly
Behind = slow down, avoid claiming, buy more rounds
Track opponents' remaining camels to anticipate game end

entries.definition: Yellow meeple. Collected and kept in front of you. Worth 10 VP each at game end plus 10 bonus VP for each opponent who has strictly fewer Viziers than you.
White meeple. Collected and kept in front of you. Worth 2 VP each at game end. Also spent as currency to purchase Djinn cards at Sacred Places.
Green meeple. When collected, take resource cards from the front of the market row equal to the number of Merchants collected.
Blue meeple. When collected, earn coins equal to the number of Builders multiplied by the number of blue VP icons on surrounding tiles including the landing tile.
Red meeple. When collected, may remove one meeple from the board within range equal to the number of Assassins collected. Alternatively, may remove one Vizier or Elder from an opponent.

The core movement mechanic. Pick up all meeples from a tile, then move orthogonally dropping one meeple per tile. Cannot backtrack to the tile you just left. Last meeple must match a color on the destination tile.
The final meeple dropped in a mancala move must match the color of at least one meeple already on the destination tile. If no match is possible, the move is illegal.
At the start of each round, all players simultaneously bid coins for turn order. Highest bidder goes first. Ties broken by previous turn order. Bid coins go to the supply permanently.
If a tile is completely empty of meeples after your move, you may place one camel on it to claim ownership. Claimed tiles score their printed VP at game end.
Blue tile. Tile action: place one palm tree on this tile. Palm trees are worth 3 VP each at game end for the player who claims the tile.
Red tile. Tile action: place one palace on this tile. Palaces are worth 5 VP each at game end for the player who claims the tile.
Tile action: draw the top 3 resource cards from the deck, keep 1, discard the rest to the bottom of the deck.
Tile action: draw the top 6 resource cards from the deck, keep 2, discard the rest to the bottom of the deck.
Orange tile. Tile action: spend 2 Elders to take 1 Djinn card from the face-up display. Some abilities allow alternate payment methods.
Wooden token placed on Oasis tiles via tile action. Worth 3 VP at game end for the player who claims the tile the palm tree sits on.
Wooden token placed on Village tiles via tile action. Worth 5 VP at game end for the player who claims the tile the palace sits on.
Player marker placed on empty tiles to claim ownership. Each player has 8 camels. The game ends when a player places their last camel.
Powerful card purchased at Sacred Places by spending 2 Elders. Grants unique ongoing powers and is worth printed VP at game end. 22 unique Djinns in the game.
Cards representing trade goods including Ivory, Jewels, Gold, Papyrus, Silk, Spices, Fish, Wheat, and Pottery. Collected via Merchant actions and Market tiles. Sold in sets for coins.
Selling prices for unique resource card sets: 1 card = 1 coin, 2 = 3, 3 = 7, 4 = 13, 5 = 21, 6 = 30, 7 = 40, 8 = 50, 9 unique = 60 coins.
Track where players place bid markers each round. Determines play order based on bid amount. First round order is random; subsequent rounds are determined by bidding.
Gold tokens in 1 and 5 denominations. Worth 1 VP each at game end. Used for bidding on turn order. Coin totals are hidden information.
Movement in straight lines: up, down, left, or right. No diagonal movement is allowed during the mancala move in Five Tribes.
One of the 30 double-sided tiles that form the 5x6 grid of the Sultanate of Naqala. Each tile has a type and a printed VP value.
At game end, each player scores 10 bonus VP for each opponent who has strictly fewer Viziers. In a 4-player game, having the most Viziers yields 30 bonus VP.
The game ends when a player places their last camel OR no legal mancala move can be made. The current round is completed so all players who bid get to take a turn.

Eight categories summed: Coins (1 VP each), Viziers (10 VP + majority), Elders (2 VP each), Claimed tiles (printed VP), Palm trees (3 VP), Palaces (5 VP), Djinns (printed VP), Unsold merchandise (converted to coins).
If tied on total VP, the player with the most Viziers plus Elders combined wins. If still tied, the player with the most resource cards wins.
The range for an Assassin kill equals the number of Assassin meeples collected. A range of 3 means you can target any meeple up to 3 tiles away orthogonally.
The face-up display of 9 resource cards next to the deck. Merchants take cards from the front of this row. Refilled to 9 cards at the end of each round.
Three face-up Djinn cards available for purchase at Sacred Places. Refilled to 3 at the end of each round from the Djinn deck.
